critters.name: White-tailed Deer
critters.summary: Green-season regulars now cruising browse lines like the salad bar finally quit pretending it was closed.
critters.fact: White-tailed deer use their tail as a warning signal by flashing its white underside.
critters.activeFact: Right now, deer should be easier to pattern around trail edges, clover patches, openings, and quiet green lanes during morning and evening.

critters.name: Black Bear
critters.summary: Big spring appetite on four paws, roaming wider now that the woods is finally stocking the shelves.
critters.fact: Black bears have an excellent sense of smell, even better than bloodhounds.
critters.activeFact: Right now, bear sign is more likely near wet ground, old logs, food-rich edges, and quiet travel routes where spring snacks pile up.

critters.name: Coyote
critters.summary: Edge-country night manager still working routes with that suspiciously organized wild-dog confidence.
critters.fact: Coyotes communicate using over 11 different vocalizations, including howls, yips, and barks.
critters.activeFact: Right now, coyotes may be moving with purpose at dawn, dusk, and overnight, especially along lanes where rabbits and rodents are active.

critters.name: Red Fox
critters.summary: Quick-footed mouse detective listening to the grass like every rustle is a confession.
critters.fact: Red foxes use their bushy tails for balance and as warm blankets in cold weather.
critters.activeFact: Right now, red foxes may cross openings quickly, pause near tall grass, and make repeated trips through reliable hunting pockets.

critters.name: Gray Fox
critters.summary: Brush-country ghost keeping the leafy side streets while the loud crowd works the open roads.
critters.fact: Gray foxes are excellent climbers, often escape predators by climbing trees—unique among North American canids.
critters.activeFact: Right now, gray fox activity is most likely tucked along thickets, brush lines, and wooded edges where new leaves help hide movement.

critters.name: Bobcat
critters.summary: Silent spring shadow moving through cover like the forest signed a non-disclosure agreement.
critters.fact: Bobcats can jump up to 10 feet high, making them excellent climbers and hunters.
critters.activeFact: Right now, bobcats may be slipping through thick transitions, downed timber, and quiet trail crossings where prey traffic is steady.

critters.name: Porcupine
critters.summary: Slow-motion leaf muncher now adding greener snacks to the usual bark-and-bud buffet.
critters.fact: Porcupines’ quills detach easily to protect them from predators, causing pain and irritation.
critters.activeFact: Right now, porcupines may be found near leafy browse, low branches, tree trunks, and quiet cover where nobody rushes the dining experience.

critters.name: Beaver
critters.summary: Creek contractor fully into spring operations, still convinced water levels require constant supervision.
critters.fact: Beavers build dams to create ponds, providing protection from predators and creating wetland habitats.
critters.activeFact: Right now, look for fresh chips, peeled sticks, muddy slides, scent mounds, and repaired water-control spots around ponds and creeks.

critters.name: Muskrat
critters.summary: Pond commuter cruising the shoreline like it has errands, appointments, and zero interest in paperwork.
critters.fact: Muskrats build dome-shaped lodges from vegetation, often with underwater entrances to protect against predators.
critters.activeFact: Right now, muskrats may be swimming regular routes, feeding near cattails, and showing up around soft banks and shallow vegetation.

critters.name: Mink
critters.summary: Creek-side little menace patrolling frog season with the focus of a snack-powered detective.
critters.fact: Minks have partially webbed feet, making them excellent swimmers and skilled hunters in water.
critters.activeFact: Right now, mink may be working pond banks, creek edges, muddy crossings, and shoreline cover where frogs and small prey are active.

critters.name: Fisher
critters.summary: Deep-woods prowler moving through greened-up timber like it knows the back roads better than the map.
critters.fact: Fishers are skilled climbers and use trees to hunt prey like birds and squirrels.
critters.activeFact: Right now, fishers may stay mostly unseen, with tracks or camera hits more likely than a clean daytime sighting.

critters.name: River Otter
critters.summary: Waterway troublemaker turning creek runs and pond edges into one long slippery victory lap.
critters.fact: River otters can hold their breath underwater for up to eight minutes while hunting fish and crustaceans.
critters.activeFact: Right now, otters may cruise channels, slide muddy banks, and work pond or creek edges where fish and amphibians are moving.

critters.name: Eastern Gray Squirrel
critters.summary: Branch-line chaos clerk still running feeder raids, nest business, and squirrel arguments before lunch.
critters.fact: Eastern Gray Squirrels use over 10,000 different sounds to communicate with each other.
critters.activeFact: Right now, gray squirrels are busy in leafy treetops, at feeders, and along branches where every chase looks personal.

critters.name: American Red Squirrel
critters.summary: Tiny red alarm siren with a forest-sized attitude and no indoor voice whatsoever.
critters.fact: American Red Squirrels fiercely defend their food caches against intruders to survive harsh winters.
critters.activeFact: Right now, red squirrels may be scolding from conifers, guarding favored trees, and announcing every movement like breaking news.

critters.name: Chipmunk
critters.summary: Pocket-sized trail rocket now fully deployed and apparently fueled by acorns and bad decisions.
critters.fact: Chipmunks use cheek pouches to carry food back to their burrows for storage.
critters.activeFact: Right now, chipmunks are darting around logs, rocks, wood piles, burrow holes, and trail edges like tiny woodland pinballs.

critters.name: Snowshoe Hare
critters.summary: Northern brush hopper finally getting closer to summer camouflage while the woods fills in around it.
critters.fact: Snowshoe hares change fur color seasonally, turning white in winter for camouflage in snow.
critters.activeFact: Right now, snowshoe hares are most likely tucked in thick young growth, brushy cover, and cool shaded spots where visibility drops fast.

critters.name: Eastern Cottontail
critters.summary: Brush-edge salad thief feeding in the open just long enough to make a dramatic exit plan.
critters.fact: Eastern Cottontails can run up to 18 miles per hour to escape predators.
critters.activeFact: Right now, cottontails may show up along lawns, trail borders, field edges, and brush piles during calm morning and evening windows.

critters.name: Raccoon
critters.summary: Night-shift snack inspector now roaming warmer evenings like every pond edge has a secret menu.
critters.fact: Raccoons have highly sensitive front paws that help them identify objects by touch in the dark.
critters.activeFact: Right now, raccoons may be hitting creeks, ponds, feeders, cabins, campsites, and muddy edges after dark with full bandit confidence.

critters.name: Opossum
critters.summary: Awkward little night wanderer finally enjoying weather that doesn’t try to freeze its toes off.
critters.fact: Opossums have a remarkable immune system that makes them resistant to many snake venoms.
critters.activeFact: Right now, opossums may be cruising brush piles, shed edges, wet ground, and quiet camp margins after sunset.

critters.name: Striped Skunk
critters.summary: Striped grub hunter working soft ground with confidence, fragrance, and absolutely no need for permission.
critters.fact: Striped skunks can spray a foul-smelling liquid up to 10 feet to deter predators.
critters.activeFact: Right now, skunks may be nosing through lawns, field edges, roadsides, and damp soil where the digging is easy.

critters.name: Flying Squirrel
critters.summary: After-dark tree glider running the silent squirrel shift while daylight chaos gets all the attention.
critters.fact: Flying squirrels glide using a flap of skin called a patagium stretching between their legs.
critters.activeFact: Right now, nighttime cameras, feeders, nest boxes, and cavities are the best bet for catching their quiet little glide raids.

critters.name: Little Brown Bat
critters.summary: Tiny dusk bug-sniper cashing in when warm evenings finally put insects on the flight schedule.
critters.fact: Little Brown Bats use echolocation to catch insects, making them excellent natural pest controllers.
critters.activeFact: Right now, mild evenings may bring them over ponds, creeks, and open corridors where insects gather after sunset.

critters.name: Big Brown Bat
critters.summary: Tougher night flier working the bug buffet while the softer crowd waits for perfect conditions.
critters.fact: Big Brown Bats can eat up to half their body weight in insects each night, helping control pests.
critters.activeFact: Right now, warm May evenings near lights, barns, cabins, and sheltered openings can bring big brown bats out on patrol.

critters.name: Barred Owl
critters.summary: Deep-woods night caller still sounding like the forest’s unpaid security system.
critters.fact: Barred Owls have distinct hoots often described as “Who cooks for you? Who cooks for you all?”
critters.activeFact: Right now, barred owl calls may carry through leafy, damp woods at dusk, dawn, and quiet overnight stretches.

critters.name: Great Horned Owl
critters.summary: Early-nesting heavyweight now deep into owl-parent mode while everyone else is still making plans.
critters.fact: Great Horned Owls can rotate their heads up to 270 degrees to spot prey without moving their bodies.
critters.activeFact: Right now, great horned owls may be quieter around nests while adults hunt hard and young birds begin getting more demanding.

critters.name: Blue Jay
critters.summary: Blue-feathered neighborhood enforcer now running nest season like a tiny angry security department.
critters.fact: Blue Jays can mimic hawk calls to scare away other birds from food sources.
critters.activeFact: Right now, blue jays are loud, territorial, and quick to start drama near feeders, nest areas, and favored trees.

critters.name: Northern Cardinal
critters.summary: Red morning announcer still posted in the brush like sunrise needs a hype man.
critters.fact: Northern Cardinals are known for their vibrant red plumage, especially in males, making them easy to spot.
critters.activeFact: Right now, cardinals are strong morning and evening singers around brush, feeders, yards, and leafy woodland edges.

critters.name: Black-capped Chickadee
critters.summary: Tiny cavity inspector now fully invested in nest business and still acting adorable about the paperwork.
critters.fact: Black-capped Chickadees can remember hundreds of hiding spots where they stash their food for later.
critters.activeFact: Right now, chickadees may be carrying nesting material, guarding cavities, and making quick food runs through leafy cover.

critters.name: Tufted Titmouse
critters.summary: Curious gray chatterbox bouncing through the canopy like it has neighborhood updates to file.
critters.fact: Tufted Titmice can mimic the calls of other birds to communicate or deceive.
critters.activeFact: Right now, titmice may be active near cavities, calling through edge trees, and moving in pairs or family zones.

critters.name: White-breasted Nuthatch
critters.summary: Upside-down bark technician still treating tree trunks like vertical sidewalks.
critters.fact: White-breasted Nuthatches can climb down trees headfirst, unlike most other birds that only climb upward.
critters.activeFact: Right now, nuthatches may be working mature trunks, calling sharply, and tending nest sites tucked into older trees.

critters.name: Downy Woodpecker
critters.summary: Pocket-sized drummer now hammering spring updates into every useful limb and dead snag.
critters.fact: Downy Woodpeckers drum on trees to communicate and establish territory, creating a unique rhythmic sound.
critters.activeFact: Right now, downies may be drumming, feeding young or preparing nests, and working smaller limbs near woods and feeders.

critters.name: Hairy Woodpecker
critters.summary: Bigger bark hammer bringing the heavy peck-work to older trees and deeper woods.
critters.fact: Hairy Woodpeckers drum on trees to communicate and find insects hidden beneath the bark.
critters.activeFact: Right now, hairy woodpeckers may be drumming and feeding around mature timber, dead limbs, and nesting cavities.

critters.name: Pileated Woodpecker
critters.summary: Giant forest carpenter still announcing itself like a red-crested jackhammer with wings.
critters.fact: Pileated Woodpeckers create large, rectangular holes in trees to find insects and build nests.
critters.activeFact: Right now, loud calls, deep pounding, and fresh rectangular holes can signal pileated woodpecker activity in bigger timber.

critters.name: Red-bellied Woodpecker
critters.summary: Noisy woodland hammerhead turning nest season into a bark-side shouting match.
critters.fact: Red-bellied Woodpeckers drum loudly to establish territory and attract mates.
critters.activeFact: Right now, they may be calling, chasing rivals, checking cavities, and working trunks near feeders and edge woods.

critters.name: Mourning Dove
critters.summary: Soft-voiced twig minimalist still proving confidence can hold a questionable nest together.
critters.fact: Mourning Doves can fly up to 55 miles per hour, making them fast and agile fliers.
critters.activeFact: Right now, paired doves may be nesting, cooing, feeding on open ground, and making awkward landings look intentional.

critters.name: American Goldfinch
critters.summary: Little yellow upgrade package getting brighter while pretending it didn’t just spend winter looking muted.
critters.fact: American Goldfinches molt twice a year, changing bright yellow feathers to duller colors in winter.
critters.activeFact: Right now, males are brightening up and getting livelier around feeders, weeds, and open edges, though nesting is still ahead.

critters.name: Dark-eyed Junco
critters.summary: Cool-weather ground feeder mostly heading out of the spotlight as May hands the stage to summer birds.
critters.fact: Dark-eyed Juncos are known as "snowbirds" because they often appear after the first snowfall.
critters.activeFact: Right now, any lingering juncos are likely fewer than earlier spring, quietly picking under feeders before the warm-season crew takes over.

critters.name: House Finch
critters.summary: Social feeder singer now fully into spring romance, rooftop concerts, and questionable confidence.
critters.fact: House Finches sing complex songs and were once sold illegally as "Hollywood Finches" in pet trade.
critters.activeFact: Right now, house finches are vocal, paired up, and likely nesting or scouting close to feeders and sheltered spots.

critters.name: Purple Finch
critters.summary: Rosy woodland wanderer still drifting through like a visitor who refuses to text before arriving.
critters.fact: Purple Finches have a sweet, warbling song that's often mistaken for a robin's cheerful tune.
critters.activeFact: Right now, purple finches may appear in short waves around feeders or forest edges before shifting deeper into breeding habitat.

critters.name: Wild Turkey
critters.summary: Strut-season circus bird still acting like every clearing is a runway and every hen is judging.
critters.fact: Wild turkeys can fly up to 55 miles per hour in short bursts to escape predators.
critters.activeFact: Right now, gobblers may still strut and gobble, while hens are more secretive as nesting pressure increases.

critters.name: Ruffed Grouse
critters.summary: Hidden forest drummer still thumping from cover like the woods installed a mystery bass drum.
critters.fact: Ruffed Grouse create "drumming" sounds by rapidly beating air with their wings to attract mates.
critters.activeFact: Right now, drumming may still roll through young woods and thick cover, especially during calm morning hours.

critters.name: Sandhill Crane
critters.summary: Tall marsh monarch calling across the landscape like it paid for the whole echo system.
critters.fact: Sandhill Cranes perform elaborate, synchronized dances as part of their courtship and social bonding rituals.
critters.activeFact: Right now, crane pairs may be territorial around wetlands, feeding in open areas, and calling loudly enough to erase subtlety.

critters.name: American Crow
critters.summary: Black-feathered property auditor still investigating every suspicious noise, snack, and bad decision.
critters.fact: American Crows can recognize and remember human faces to avoid potential threats.
critters.activeFact: Right now, crows may be extra watchful around nest sites, quick to mob raptors, and loud enough to file verbal complaints.

critters.name: Common Raven
critters.summary: Big brainy sky goth cruising above the trees like it knows where the weird stuff is buried.
critters.fact: Common Ravens can mimic sounds from their environment, including human speech and other animal calls.
critters.activeFact: Right now, ravens may be moving in pairs or family territory, calling over timber, and patrolling familiar routes with purpose.

critters.name: Red-tailed Hawk
critters.summary: Airborne field boss balancing nest duty with that classic raptor stare of total disappointment.
critters.fact: Red-tailed Hawks use soaring flight to spot prey from high altitudes with exceptional vision.
critters.activeFact: Right now, red-tails may be circling near nest areas, perching over fields, and watching open ground for careless movement.

critters.name: Cooper’s Hawk
critters.summary: Woodland speed demon slicing through trees like songbirds just heard boss music.
critters.fact: Cooper’s Hawks are agile hunters, expertly navigating dense trees to catch birds mid-flight.
critters.activeFact: Right now, sudden panic at the feeders or a quick blur through branches may mean a Cooper’s hawk is on patrol.

critters.name: Mallard Duck
critters.summary: Classic pond duck now fully invested in May water, nesting cover, and whatever shallow spot looks useful.
critters.fact: Mallard ducks are experts at adapting to urban environments and can thrive in city parks worldwide.
critters.activeFact: Right now, mallards may be paired, nesting, loafing, or feeding around ponds, flooded pockets, and quiet shoreline cover.

critters.name: Wood Duck
critters.summary: Flashy backwater jewel still slipping through wooded water like it has a private entrance.
critters.fact: Wood Ducks are excellent tree nesters and often use abandoned woodpecker holes for their nests.
critters.activeFact: Right now, wood ducks may be nesting in cavities or moving quietly through pond edges, flooded timber, and sheltered creek bends.

critters.name: Canada Goose
critters.summary: Pondside bouncer now guarding nesting territory like everyone else is automatically trespassing.
critters.fact: Canada Geese can fly thousands of miles during migration using a distinctive V-formation.
critters.activeFact: Right now, geese may be nesting or guarding young around ponds and fields, so their attitude is not a software glitch.

critters.name: Great Blue Heron
critters.summary: Shoreline statue with a spear face, patiently waiting for fish to make one poor life choice.
critters.fact: Great Blue Herons use their sharp beaks to spear fish with incredible speed and precision.
critters.activeFact: Right now, herons may hunt pond edges, creek shallows, marsh margins, and any quiet water holding frogs or fish.

critters.name: Painted Turtle
critters.summary: Little pond solar panel now stacking logs every time May sunshine punches the clock.
critters.fact: Painted turtles can absorb oxygen through their skin while underwater, helping them stay submerged longer.
critters.activeFact: Right now, sunny logs and banks may be loaded with painted turtles soaking heat between feeding and moving through shallow water.

critters.name: Snapping Turtle
critters.summary: Old swamp tank now moving with more purpose as warmer water wakes up the heavy equipment.
critters.fact: Snapping turtles have powerful jaws that can snap shut at speeds up to 200 miles per hour.
critters.activeFact: Right now, snappers may be cruising muddy shallows, pond edges, and creek bottoms with that prehistoric bulldozer energy.

critters.name: Bluegill
critters.summary: Panfish crowd now sliding toward the shallows like the spawning neighborhood is about to open.
critters.fact: Bluegill can change their body color to communicate during mating or when threatened.
critters.activeFact: Right now, bluegill may be staging near warm shallows, weeds, wood, and protected edges where nest-building season is getting close.

critters.name: Largemouth Bass
critters.summary: Big-mouthed shallow lurker now acting like every weed edge might become real estate.
critters.fact: Largemouth bass can rapidly change color to blend with their surroundings while hunting.
critters.activeFact: Right now, bass may be cruising, staging, or locking onto warmer shallows, wood, weeds, and quiet nesting pockets.

critters.name: Yellow Perch
critters.summary: Striped school fish pushing through post-spawn spring water like it finally has places to be.
critters.fact: Yellow Perch have distinct dark vertical stripes, which help them blend into underwater vegetation for protection.
critters.activeFact: Right now, perch may be moving in groups through nearshore weeds and recovering into stronger feeding patterns after early spring spawning.

critters.name: Northern Pike
critters.summary: Toothy green missile still haunting shallow weed lines like it owns the ambush rights.
critters.fact: Northern Pike can grow over 4 feet long and are known for their sharp teeth and aggressive hunting style.
critters.activeFact: Right now, pike may patrol flooded edges, weeds, creek mouths, and shallow ambush lanes where baitfish wander too casually.

critters.name: Spring Peeper
critters.summary: Tiny frog alarm system still turning wet May nights into a woodland speaker test.
critters.fact: Spring Peepers have a loud, high-pitched call that signals the start of spring in many North American forests.
critters.activeFact: Right now, warm damp evenings can still trigger loud peeper choruses, especially near low wet pockets and pond edges.

critters.name: American Bullfrog
critters.summary: Big bass-drum frog finally warming up for the deeper pond soundtrack.
critters.fact: American bullfrogs can produce loud, deep calls that carry over half a mile through water and wetlands.
critters.activeFact: Right now, bullfrogs may begin showing at pond edges and warmer shallows, though their biggest chorus days are still building.

critters.name: Green Frog
critters.summary: Shoreline frog now joining the pond conversation with a loose banjo-plunk attitude.
critters.fact: Green frogs can produce a distinctive "gunk" sound to communicate during mating season.
critters.activeFact: Right now, green frogs may be showing and calling around calm banks, pond margins, wet pockets, and sunny shallows.

critters.name: Leopard Frog
critters.summary: Spotted spring jumper working wet grass and pond edges like the whole place is a launch pad.
critters.fact: Leopard frogs have distinctive dark spots and can jump over 10 times their body length in one leap.
critters.activeFact: Right now, leopard frogs may be active around puddles, ditches, marsh edges, and wet grass after warm rain.

critters.name: Garter Snake
critters.summary: Striped sun-rope now fully back on patrol wherever frogs, warmth, and bad luck overlap.
critters.fact: Garter snakes can detect chemicals in the air using their forked tongue and Jacobson's organ.
critters.activeFact: Right now, garter snakes may be basking on trails, hunting near wet edges, and slipping through grass where frogs are active.

critters.name: Water Snake
critters.summary: Bank-side tough guy now warming into pond duty and ready to ruin a fish’s afternoon.
critters.fact: Water snakes can hold their breath underwater for up to 30 minutes while hunting or hiding.
critters.activeFact: Right now, water snakes may be basking on sunny banks, logs, rocks, or creek edges near calm water.

critters.name: Eastern Milk Snake
critters.summary: Secretive cover crawler now moving more often where old boards, barns, and warm edges hold heat.
critters.fact: Eastern Milk Snakes mimic venomous rattlesnakes by vibrating their tails to scare predators away.
critters.activeFact: Right now, milk snakes may turn up under warm cover, near wood piles, or along brushy edges during mild weather.

critters.name: Monarch Butterfly
critters.summary: Orange traveler starting to feel less like a rumor as milkweed season creeps closer.
critters.fact: Monarch butterflies migrate thousands of miles annually, traveling from North America to central Mexico.
critters.activeFact: Right now, monarch activity may still be limited in northern Michigan, but incoming migrants and milkweed growth are the next signs to watch.

critters.name: Swallowtail Butterfly
critters.summary: Big-winged warm-weather showoff finally getting closer to regular stage time.
critters.fact: Swallowtail butterflies can mimic toxic species to avoid predators, showcasing nature's clever survival tactics.
critters.activeFact: Right now, swallowtails may start appearing more often on warm, sunny afternoons as blossoms and leaf cover increase.

critters.name: Dragonfly
critters.summary: Sky hunter loading in from the pond world as warmer water starts unlocking the aerial patrol.
critters.fact: Dragonflies can fly straight up, down, hover, and even backwards with incredible speed and agility.
critters.activeFact: Right now, early dragonfly activity may begin near sunny wetlands and ponds, while many are still developing below the surface.

critters.name: Damselfly
critters.summary: Delicate pond-edge flyer beginning to trade underwater hiding for reed-line hovering.
critters.fact: Damselflies can see nearly 360 degrees with their large, multifaceted compound eyes.
critters.activeFact: Right now, early damselflies may begin appearing near calm pond margins, reeds, and sunny shoreline vegetation.

critters.name: Firefly
critters.summary: Summer light crew still mostly backstage, but the curtain is starting to twitch.
critters.fact: Fireflies use bioluminescence to communicate, especially for attracting mates during warm summer nights.
critters.activeFact: Right now, fireflies are still mostly waiting for warmer nights, but late May and early summer are sneaking closer.

critters.name: Honeybee
critters.summary: Busy flower accountant now making regular rounds whenever the weather stops acting dramatic.
critters.fact: Honeybees communicate by performing a waggle dance to show others where to find food.
critters.activeFact: Right now, honeybees may be working early flowers, fruit blossoms, dandelions, and warm sunny edges around camp.

critters.name: Bumblebee
critters.summary: Fuzzy queen-powered workhorse now scouting flowers and nest spots like she’s building a tiny empire.
critters.fact: Bumblebees can regulate their body temperature to fly in cold weather.
critters.activeFact: Right now, bumblebees are more common on warm days, especially around blooming weeds, shrubs, and low sunny ground.

critters.name: Grasshopper
critters.summary: Jump-season rookie still mostly warming up while the frogs and bees hog May’s spotlight.
critters.fact: Grasshoppers can jump over 20 times their body length using powerful hind legs.
critters.activeFact: Right now, grasshopper activity is still limited, but warmer sunny grass and field edges are starting to build their season.
